⬡ Ash · Devices Contents Cheatsheet
⬡ A BookBank field guide

AshDevices

A framework for modeling, simulating, and operating cyber-physical systems with the Elixir Ash Framework — where robots, cameras, doors, and lab instruments become declarative resources, hardware is just an adapter underneath, and the simulator is a first-class runtime. This is the engineering philosophy, the architecture, and a grounded look at how you'd actually build it on today's Ash & Nerves ecosystem.

in the voice of Richard Feynman · researched from the live Ash, Spark & Elixir-hardware ecosystem

▶ Start reading ⚡ Cheatsheet

Before you turn the page

Ash made business logic declarative: you describe resources, actions, and policies, and the framework generates the plumbing. AshDevices asks the obvious next question — what if a physical machine could be described the same way, so that a warehouse robot felt as ordinary to program as a database row?

This book has two jobs. First, to lay out the AshDevices philosophy faithfully — its core idea that hardware is an implementation detail and the resource is the product, plus the architectural rules that fall out of it. Second — the part you asked for most — to do the research and find the stunning use cases: the labs, factories, farms, and buildings that get easier when a device is a resource you can simulate, inspect, and replay.

🧭
How to read this

Chapters 1–2 set the idea and the mindset. Chapters 3–6 build the architecture: resources, capabilities, adapters, then intent/state/events. Chapter 7 is the heart — simulation-first. Chapters 8–9 cover the craft and the Studio. Chapter 10 is the payoff: the use cases. Chapter 11 leaves you with a checklist to decide by.

cover ·

Table of Contents

  1. Hardware as Software The one sentence behind AshDevices — and why "declarative" is the whole trick for a robot, too. the why
  2. Stop Thinking in Wires Never reason in GPIO, MQTT, or Modbus. Reason in resources, capabilities, and actions. philosophy
  3. Everything Is a Resource What Ash already gives you — and why a Robot, a Door, and an Experiment are all just resources. foundations
  4. Composition Over Inheritance No deep class trees. Behavior is assembled from reusable Capabilities a resource opts into. composition
  5. Domain Before Protocol: Adapters Resources define intent; adapters execute it. Swap the hardware without touching the app. adapters
  6. Intent, State & Events Actions as business operations, explicit state machines, first-class events, and digital twins. runtime
  7. Simulation First The simulator is the default runtime, not an afterthought. Build, test, and demo with no hardware. simulation
  8. The Craft: APIs, Names & Errors APIs that read like English, names a scientist would recognize, and errors you can act on. standards
  9. The Studio: Seeing the Runtime A development environment, not a dashboard — every pixel maps to a real runtime concept. tooling
  10. Stunning Use Cases Self-driving labs, lights-out factories, smart farms, buildings, drones, and warehouses. payoff
  11. The Decision Checklist Ten questions to ask before writing any feature — and the one principle that ties it together. closing
cover ·
🔑
About this book

Built from the AshDevices Engineering Guide for Coding Agents — a design manifesto for a framework that turns physical devices into Ash resources — then grounded against the real ecosystem: Ash & Spark (the DSL toolkit behind Ash), AshStateMachine, Ash notifiers & AshEvents, and the Elixir hardware stack — Nerves, Circuits.GPIO/I2C/UART, and MQTT/Modbus clients. Where AshDevices itself is a proposal rather than a shipped package, the book says so plainly. Everything renders offline.

🧩
A note on scope

The request arrived as a long engineering guide that BookBank split into hundreds of fragments — every heading, bullet, and arrow became its own "concept." Rather than make a page per fragment, this guide reorganizes the whole manifesto into eleven chapters that actually read like a book — keeping every idea, losing none of the substance, and adding the use-case research you asked for.

Press ⌘R or reopen the book to refresh after edits.

⌂ Library
